abstraction.focus: hiding implementation details behind a simple public interface
abstraction.use: It helps callers use a feature without knowing every internal step.
abstraction.whyOutput: The caller only uses send(); the details of building and delivering the email stay inside the class.
abstraction.mistake: exposing every internal helper as public API
abstraction.practice: keep the outside method simple and move noisy details into private helpers

apply.focus: calling a function with a chosen this value and arguments passed as an array
apply.use: It is useful when arguments already exist in array form.
apply.output: Asha from Pune works as dev
apply.whyOutput: apply sets this to the object and spreads the array values into the function parameters.
apply.mistake: passing normal comma arguments to apply instead of an array
apply.practice: use call for comma arguments and apply when the arguments are already collected

arrow-functions.focus: short function syntax with lexical this binding
arrow-functions.use: It makes callbacks cleaner and avoids rebinding this inside nested functions.
arrow-functions.whyOutput: map calls the arrow callback for every number and returns the doubled values.
arrow-functions.mistake: using arrow functions as constructors or expecting their own this
arrow-functions.practice: use arrows for callbacks and small transforms, not for methods that need dynamic this

async-await.focus: writing Promise-based asynchronous code in a synchronous-looking style
async-await.use: It makes async flow easier to read while still running through Promises.
async-await.whyOutput: await pauses inside the async function until the Promise resolves, then the resolved value is transformed.
async-await.mistake: forgetting that an async function always returns a Promise
async-await.practice: wrap await code in try/catch when the Promise can reject

bind.focus: creating a new function with this permanently set
bind.use: It keeps methods connected to the correct object when passed around.
bind.whyOutput: bind returns a new function whose this always points to user.
bind.mistake: calling bind and expecting the original function to change
bind.practice: store or pass the bound function returned by bind

block-scope.focus: limiting variables to the nearest pair of curly braces
block-scope.use: It keeps temporary values from leaking outside if, for, and block statements.
block-scope.whyOutput: message exists only inside the if block where it was declared.
block-scope.mistake: trying to read let or const outside the block where it was declared
block-scope.practice: declare variables in the smallest block that needs them

call.focus: calling a function with a chosen this value and comma-separated arguments
call.use: It lets one function operate on different objects.
call.whyOutput: call sets this to the object and passes admin as the first parameter.
call.mistake: forgetting that call immediately invokes the function
call.practice: use call when you want to run the function right now with a custom this

callback-functions.focus: passing a function as a value so another function can run it later
callback-functions.use: It lets you customize behavior without rewriting the outer function.
callback-functions.whyOutput: calculate receives the callback and calls it with 4 and 3.
callback-functions.mistake: calling the callback while passing it instead of passing the function reference
callback-functions.practice: name callbacks clearly when the logic is more than a one-line expression

callback-hell.focus: deeply nested callbacks that make async code hard to read and handle
callback-hell.use: It appears when each async step is written inside the previous step.
callback-hell.whyOutput: each callback runs the next step, creating a nested chain.
callback-hell.mistake: handling errors separately at every nesting level until the code becomes unreadable
callback-hell.practice: prefer Promise chains or async/await for multi-step async flows

callback-queue.focus: the queue where timer and browser callback tasks wait before entering the call stack
callback-queue.use: It explains why setTimeout callbacks run only after synchronous code finishes.
callback-queue.whyOutput: the synchronous log runs first; the timer callback waits in the callback queue.
callback-queue.mistake: thinking 0 milliseconds means immediate execution before current code
callback-queue.practice: remember that queued callbacks wait until the call stack is empty

callbacks.focus: functions handed to another function to be executed at the right moment
callbacks.use: They power events, array methods, timers, and many async APIs.
callbacks.whyOutput: map calls the callback once for each name and collects the returned values.
callbacks.mistake: mutating external state inside callbacks when a returned value would be clearer
callbacks.practice: keep callbacks small and move complex callback logic into named functions

cheat-sheets.focus: compact reference notes for syntax, rules, and common patterns
cheat-sheets.use: They help revise quickly after the concept is already understood.
cheat-sheets.syntax:
// Example cheat note
// const: block scoped, no reassignment
cheat-sheets.example:
const rule = "const needs initial value";
console.log(rule.includes("const"));
cheat-sheets.whyOutput: includes checks whether the cheat-note string contains the searched word.
cheat-sheets.mistake: using cheat sheets as a replacement for practicing real examples
cheat-sheets.practice: keep cheat sheets short, accurate, and connected to runnable snippets

classes.focus: JavaScript syntax for creating objects with shared methods
classes.use: Classes organize constructor logic, methods, inheritance, and encapsulation.
classes.whyOutput: new creates an object, constructor stores the name, and greet reads it.
classes.mistake: forgetting new when creating a class instance
classes.practice: use classes when objects share behavior, not just to group unrelated functions

closures.focus: a function remembering variables from the scope where it was created
closures.use: They enable private state, function factories, and stable callbacks.
closures.whyOutput: the returned function keeps access to count even after counter has finished.
closures.mistake: assuming the remembered variable is copied instead of shared by the closure
closures.practice: use closures deliberately for private state and avoid hidden mutable state

coding-challenges.focus: practice problems that test syntax, logic, edge cases, and debugging
coding-challenges.use: They turn concepts into real problem-solving skill.
coding-challenges.whyOutput: split creates characters, reverse changes their order, and join rebuilds the string.
coding-challenges.mistake: solving only the happy path and skipping empty or unusual inputs
coding-challenges.practice: write a few test cases before considering a challenge solved

commonjs.focus: Node.js module syntax based on require and module.exports
commonjs.use: It lets files share values in older or CommonJS-based Node projects.
commonjs.example:
const math = { add: (a, b) => a + b };
console.log(math.add(2, 5));
commonjs.whyOutput: the exported-style object exposes add, and calling it returns the sum.
commonjs.mistake: mixing CommonJS and ES module syntax without checking project configuration
commonjs.practice: use one module style consistently inside a project

constructor-functions.focus: older function-based syntax for creating similar objects with new
constructor-functions.use: It explains how objects were commonly built before class syntax.
constructor-functions.whyOutput: new creates an object and binds this inside Person to that object.
constructor-functions.mistake: calling a constructor function without new and losing the intended this
constructor-functions.practice: prefer class syntax in modern code unless reading older code

cookies.focus: small browser-stored key-value data sent with matching HTTP requests
cookies.use: They are commonly used for sessions, preferences, and server-readable state.
cookies.whyOutput: splitting the cookie-like string reads the first key-value pair.
cookies.mistake: putting sensitive data in readable client-side cookies
cookies.practice: use secure, httpOnly, and sameSite options for real authentication cookies

currying.focus: turning a multi-argument function into a chain of single-argument functions
currying.use: It helps pre-fill values and compose reusable specialized functions.
currying.whyOutput: multiply(2) returns a new function that remembers 2 and multiplies the next value.
currying.mistake: making simple code harder by currying when partial application is not needed
currying.practice: use currying when it clearly improves reuse or composition

debouncing.focus: delaying a function until rapid repeated calls stop
debouncing.use: It is useful for search input, resize handlers, and expensive live updates.
debouncing.whyOutput: a real debounce would cancel earlier scheduled calls and keep only the final one.
debouncing.mistake: debouncing actions that should respond immediately, like critical clicks
debouncing.practice: choose a delay that feels responsive but prevents unnecessary work

deep-copy.focus: copying nested objects so inner objects are not shared by reference
deep-copy.use: It prevents changes in the copy from mutating the original nested data.
deep-copy.whyOutput: structuredClone creates a separate nested profile object, so the original city stays unchanged.
deep-copy.mistake: using spread syntax and assuming it copies every nested level
deep-copy.practice: use structuredClone or a deliberate copy strategy for nested data

destructuring.focus: extracting object properties or array items into variables
destructuring.use: It makes reads from structured data concise and clear.
destructuring.whyOutput: destructuring reads name and role from the object into local variables.
destructuring.mistake: destructuring from undefined without a fallback
destructuring.practice: use defaults when a property or parameter may be missing

dom.focus: the browser's object representation of an HTML document
dom.use: It lets JavaScript read and change page structure, text, and attributes.
dom.whyOutput: the object represents a DOM-like node whose textContent can be changed.
dom.mistake: touching the DOM before the element exists
dom.practice: run DOM code after the page or component has mounted

dom-events.focus: browser signals such as clicks, input changes, submits, and key presses
dom-events.use: They let JavaScript react to user interaction.
dom-events.whyOutput: the handler receives the event type and returns a response for that interaction.
dom-events.mistake: attaching many listeners and never removing them when elements disappear
dom-events.practice: keep event handlers small and clean them up when needed

dom-manipulation.focus: changing DOM nodes, text, attributes, classes, or structure with JavaScript
dom-manipulation.use: It is how scripts update what the user sees.
dom-manipulation.whyOutput: the object stands in for a DOM node whose displayed text was changed.
dom-manipulation.mistake: manually changing DOM that a framework like React is also controlling
dom-manipulation.practice: let the framework own UI updates when using React or Next.js

encapsulation.focus: keeping data and behavior together while limiting direct access
encapsulation.use: It protects object state from random outside mutation.
encapsulation.whyOutput: the private balance changes only through the deposit method.
encapsulation.mistake: making fields public when they should be changed only through rules
encapsulation.practice: expose methods that describe allowed actions instead of exposing raw state

es-modules.focus: modern JavaScript module syntax using import and export
es-modules.use: It enables static analysis, tree shaking, and cleaner browser or bundler modules.
es-modules.syntax:
export const value = 1;
import { value } from "./file.js";
es-modules.whyOutput: the example shows named import syntax for a named export.
es-modules.mistake: forgetting file extensions or module type rules in Node environments
es-modules.practice: prefer named exports for shared utilities and default exports for one main value

event-bubbling.focus: an event traveling from the target element up through its ancestors
event-bubbling.use: It lets parent elements observe events from children.
event-bubbling.syntax: child click -> parent click -> document click
event-bubbling.whyOutput: the event starts at the clicked button and then bubbles to the parent card.
event-bubbling.mistake: forgetting that parent click handlers can run after child clicks
event-bubbling.practice: use stopPropagation only when bubbling would create incorrect behavior

event-capturing.focus: an event traveling from ancestors down to the target before bubbling
event-capturing.use: It lets outer elements intercept an event early.
event-capturing.whyOutput: capturing visits outer ancestors before the final target.
event-capturing.mistake: using capture without realizing it changes handler order
event-capturing.practice: use capture only when you need early interception

event-delegation.focus: handling child events from one shared parent listener
event-delegation.use: It is efficient for lists with many or changing child elements.
event-delegation.whyOutput: the parent-style handler checks the clicked target and decides the action.
event-delegation.mistake: assuming event.target is always the exact element you expect
event-delegation.practice: check selectors or data attributes before acting on delegated events

event-loop.focus: the coordination system between the call stack, microtasks, and macrotasks
event-loop.use: It explains JavaScript's async execution order.
event-loop.whyOutput: sync code runs first, Promise callbacks run as microtasks, and timers run later as macrotasks.
event-loop.mistake: expecting timers to run before resolved Promise callbacks
event-loop.practice: learn the order: stack first, microtasks next, macrotasks after that

fetch-api.focus: the browser API for making HTTP requests with Promises
fetch-api.use: It is commonly used to load JSON or send data to servers.
fetch-api.syntax:
const response = await fetch(url);
const data = await response.json();
fetch-api.whyOutput: the async function returns the simulated response status as a Promise result.
fetch-api.mistake: forgetting that fetch rejects on network errors but not automatically on HTTP 404
fetch-api.practice: check response.ok before trusting the response body

function-scope.focus: variables being visible throughout the function where they are declared
function-scope.use: It explains why var ignores block boundaries but stays inside functions.
function-scope.whyOutput: var is scoped to the function, so it can be read after the if block.
function-scope.mistake: expecting var to behave like let inside blocks
function-scope.practice: prefer let and const for block scope in modern code

functions.focus: reusable blocks of code that accept input and return output
functions.use: They organize logic and prevent repeating the same steps.
functions.whyOutput: the function receives Neha as an argument and returns a greeting string.
functions.mistake: doing too many unrelated things inside one function
functions.practice: keep functions focused and name them by what they return or perform

garbage-collection.focus: automatic cleanup of memory that is no longer reachable
garbage-collection.use: It helps JavaScript reclaim objects your program can no longer use.
garbage-collection.whyOutput: once no reachable reference points to the object, it becomes collectible later.
garbage-collection.mistake: believing you can force exact garbage collection timing in normal code
garbage-collection.practice: remove references from long-lived structures when data is no longer needed

generators.focus: functions that can pause with yield and resume later
generators.use: They create custom iterators and lazy sequences.
generators.whyOutput: each next call resumes the generator until the next yield.
generators.mistake: forgetting that calling a generator returns an iterator, not the final value
generators.practice: use generators when laziness or pausing makes the flow clearer

higher-order-functions.focus: functions that receive functions or return functions
higher-order-functions.use: They make transformation, filtering, and composition reusable.
higher-order-functions.whyOutput: withLogging returns a new function that wraps the original callback.
higher-order-functions.mistake: nesting higher-order functions until the flow becomes hard to follow
higher-order-functions.practice: use them when they remove duplication, not to look clever

iife.focus: a function that is defined and immediately executed
iife.use: It was often used to create a private scope before modules were common.
iife.whyOutput: the function runs immediately and returns the computed value.
iife.mistake: using IIFEs where a normal block or module scope would be simpler
iife.practice: use IIFEs only when immediate private setup is actually useful

inheritance.focus: sharing behavior from a parent class or prototype with child objects
inheritance.use: It avoids duplicating common methods across related types.
inheritance.whyOutput: Dog inherits speak from Animal through extends.
inheritance.mistake: creating deep inheritance chains that are hard to reason about
inheritance.practice: prefer shallow inheritance and composition when relationships are not truly is-a

iterators.focus: objects that provide values one at a time through next()
iterators.use: They power for...of, generators, arrays, maps, and custom sequences.
iterators.whyOutput: each next call returns the next item from the array iterator.
iterators.mistake: forgetting that an iterator can be consumed and may not restart
iterators.practice: create a new iterator when you need to loop from the beginning again

javascript-interview-questions.focus: concept checks that test both definitions and execution reasoning
javascript-interview-questions.use: They help prepare answers that include examples, edge cases, and dry runs.
javascript-interview-questions.syntax: // Explain concept -> show code -> predict output
javascript-interview-questions.whyOutput: the answer string contains the key idea interviewers expect for closures.
javascript-interview-questions.mistake: memorizing definitions without being able to dry-run code
javascript-interview-questions.practice: answer with a short definition, one example, and why the output happens

lexical-environment.focus: the internal structure that stores bindings and links to outer scopes
lexical-environment.use: It explains how JavaScript resolves variable names.
lexical-environment.syntax: inner environment -> outer environment -> global environment
lexical-environment.whyOutput: JavaScript first finds inner in the current lexical environment.
lexical-environment.mistake: confusing lexical location with where a function is called
lexical-environment.practice: read scope based on where code is written, not only where it runs

local-storage.focus: persistent browser key-value storage that stays after reloads
local-storage.use: It is useful for non-sensitive preferences and small client-side state.
local-storage.whyOutput: the Map simulates storing and reading a localStorage-style key.
local-storage.mistake: storing tokens or sensitive personal data in localStorage
local-storage.practice: store only small non-sensitive values and parse JSON safely

macrotask-queue.focus: the task queue for timers, events, and other larger async callbacks
macrotask-queue.use: It runs after synchronous code and after the current microtask queue is drained.
macrotask-queue.whyOutput: the timer is a macrotask, so it runs after sync code and microtasks.
macrotask-queue.mistake: expecting timer callbacks to beat Promise callbacks
macrotask-queue.practice: separate microtask and macrotask behavior when predicting output

mcqs.focus: multiple-choice questions that test precise JavaScript rules
mcqs.use: They are useful for spotting gaps in syntax, output, and edge cases.
mcqs.syntax: // Pick answer, then verify by running code
mcqs.whyOutput: the selected option represents what many hoisting MCQs ask about.
mcqs.mistake: guessing without explaining why the selected option is correct
mcqs.practice: after choosing an option, write one line explaining the rule behind it

memoization.focus: caching function results for repeated inputs
memoization.use: It speeds up expensive pure functions when the same input appears again.
memoization.whyOutput: the first call stores the result, and the second call reads it from cache.
memoization.mistake: memoizing functions that depend on changing external state
memoization.practice: memoize only pure or predictably keyed computations

memory-leak.focus: memory staying reachable even though the app no longer needs it
memory-leak.use: It can slow apps down over time and cause growing memory usage.
memory-leak.whyOutput: the array keeps a reference to the callback, so it cannot be cleaned up.
memory-leak.mistake: leaving timers, listeners, or caches alive forever
memory-leak.practice: clean up subscriptions and remove unused objects from long-lived collections

memory-management.focus: how JavaScript allocates, uses, and releases memory
memory-management.use: It helps you avoid leaks and reason about object lifetimes.
memory-management.syntax:
let data = { active: true };
data = null;
memory-management.whyOutput: the variable now points to a smaller array, and the old array can be freed later if unreachable.
memory-management.mistake: holding references in globals when temporary data is no longer needed
memory-management.practice: keep ownership clear and release references from long-lived places

microtask-queue.focus: the queue for Promise reactions and similar high-priority async callbacks
microtask-queue.use: It runs after the current stack and before macrotasks like timers.
microtask-queue.whyOutput: the Promise callback waits as a microtask until synchronous code finishes.
microtask-queue.mistake: forgetting that microtasks can run before setTimeout callbacks
microtask-queue.practice: check microtasks before macrotasks when predicting async output

modules.focus: splitting JavaScript code into files with explicit imports and exports
modules.use: They keep code organized and avoid relying on global variables.
modules.syntax:
export function helper() {}
import { helper } from "./helper.js";
modules.whyOutput: the example represents reading a named export from a utility module.
modules.mistake: creating circular dependencies that make initialization confusing
modules.practice: keep module boundaries clear and avoid hidden side effects during import

nullish-coalescing.focus: choosing a fallback only when a value is null or undefined
nullish-coalescing.use: It preserves valid falsy values like 0, false, and empty strings.
nullish-coalescing.whyOutput: 0 is not null or undefined, so nullish coalescing keeps it.
nullish-coalescing.mistake: using || when 0 or empty string should be treated as valid
nullish-coalescing.practice: use ?? for default values where only null and undefined mean missing

object-create.focus: creating an object with a specific prototype
object-create.use: It gives direct control over prototype linkage without calling a constructor.
object-create.whyOutput: dog does not own eats, but JavaScript finds it on the prototype object.
object-create.mistake: forgetting that inherited properties are not own properties
object-create.practice: use Object.hasOwn when you need to distinguish own and inherited properties

objects.focus: key-value collections used to model structured data and behavior
objects.use: They are the foundation for most JavaScript data structures.
objects.whyOutput: dot notation reads the name property from the object.
objects.mistake: assuming object property order should drive important logic
objects.practice: use objects for named data and choose arrays when order is the main concern

operators.focus: symbols and keywords that perform calculations, comparisons, and logical checks
operators.use: They are how expressions produce new values.
operators.syntax:
a + b
value === other
condition && result
operators.whyOutput: multiplication runs before addition because of operator precedence.
operators.mistake: forgetting precedence and reading expressions left-to-right every time
operators.practice: use parentheses when precedence is not obvious

optional-chaining.focus: safely reading nested properties that may be missing
optional-chaining.use: It prevents crashes when an intermediate value is null or undefined.
optional-chaining.whyOutput: profile is missing, so optional chaining stops and returns undefined.
optional-chaining.mistake: using optional chaining where missing data should actually be treated as an error
optional-chaining.practice: use it for truly optional data, not to hide broken assumptions

output-based-questions.focus: questions where you predict what JavaScript prints before running it
output-based-questions.use: They test execution order, coercion, scope, and async behavior.
output-based-questions.whyOutput: string concatenation happens in the first expression, numeric addition in the second.
output-based-questions.mistake: running the code before trying to reason through it
output-based-questions.practice: predict first, write the rule, then verify by running the snippet

pass-by-value-vs-reference.focus: how primitives copy values while objects copy references
pass-by-value-vs-reference.use: It explains why changing an object through one variable can affect another.
pass-by-value-vs-reference.syntax:
let b = a;
const copy = objectRef;
pass-by-value-vs-reference.whyOutput: primitive x was copied independently, but user and same point to one object.
pass-by-value-vs-reference.mistake: thinking object assignment creates a deep copy
pass-by-value-vs-reference.practice: clone objects intentionally when independent mutation is required

performance-optimization.focus: improving speed, responsiveness, and resource usage without changing behavior
performance-optimization.use: It keeps apps smooth as data and interaction grow.
performance-optimization.whyOutput: filter visits the list once and keeps only even numbers.
performance-optimization.mistake: optimizing code before measuring the actual bottleneck
performance-optimization.practice: profile first, reduce unnecessary work, and keep changes measurable

polymorphism.focus: different objects responding to the same method name in their own way
polymorphism.use: It lets code work with a shared interface instead of exact classes.
polymorphism.whyOutput: both objects expose area(), but each implementation returns its own result.
polymorphism.mistake: forcing type checks everywhere instead of using a common method
polymorphism.practice: design shared method names when objects should be interchangeable

promise-chaining.focus: connecting Promise steps with then and catch
promise-chaining.use: It passes one async result into the next transformation.
promise-chaining.whyOutput: each then receives the previous returned value and passes its result forward.
promise-chaining.mistake: forgetting to return a Promise or value from inside then
promise-chaining.practice: return each async step so the chain waits correctly

promises.focus: objects representing a future success or failure value
promises.use: They are the base abstraction behind modern JavaScript async code.
promises.whyOutput: the resolved Promise schedules the then callback with the resolved value.
promises.mistake: treating Promise results like immediate synchronous values
promises.practice: use then or await to access the eventual result

prototype.focus: the object JavaScript checks when a property is not found directly
prototype.use: It enables shared methods and property lookup through a chain.
prototype.whyOutput: role is not on child, so JavaScript looks up to parent.
prototype.mistake: confusing __proto__ with the function's prototype property
prototype.practice: use Object.getPrototypeOf for inspection and class syntax for most app code

prototype-chain.focus: the linked path JavaScript follows during property lookup
prototype-chain.use: It explains inherited methods and missing property resolution.
prototype-chain.whyOutput: JavaScript walks from child to parent to grandParent until it finds level.
prototype-chain.mistake: assuming lookup checks only one prototype level
prototype-chain.practice: know where shared methods live to debug inherited behavior

rest-parameters.focus: collecting remaining function arguments into a real array
rest-parameters.use: They make variable-length functions cleaner than arguments.
rest-parameters.whyOutput: rest parameters collect 1, 2, and 3 into the numbers array.
rest-parameters.mistake: putting another parameter after the rest parameter
rest-parameters.practice: use rest as the final parameter when a function accepts many values

scope.focus: where variables and functions can be accessed in code
scope.use: It controls name visibility and prevents unrelated code from clashing.
scope.syntax:
global scope
function scope
block scope
scope.whyOutput: inside is found in the function's local scope.
scope.mistake: expecting a local variable to be available everywhere
scope.practice: keep variables close to where they are used

scope-chain.focus: the lookup path from inner scope to outer scopes
scope-chain.use: It explains how nested functions find variables.
scope-chain.whyOutput: inner finds outerName in outer scope and globalName in global scope.
scope-chain.practice: trace lookup outward from the current scope only

session-storage.focus: browser key-value storage that lasts for the current tab session
session-storage.use: It is useful for temporary tab-specific UI state.
session-storage.whyOutput: the Map simulates storing and reading a sessionStorage-style value.
session-storage.mistake: expecting sessionStorage to persist after the tab is closed
session-storage.practice: use it for temporary state, not long-term preferences

shallow-copy.focus: copying only the first level of an object or array
shallow-copy.use: It keeps nested objects shared between the original and the copy.
shallow-copy.whyOutput: the top object was copied, but profile still points to the same nested object.
shallow-copy.mistake: assuming spread creates a deep independent clone
shallow-copy.practice: use shallow copies for flat data and deep copies for nested mutation

spread-operator.focus: expanding arrays, objects, or iterables into another place
spread-operator.use: It helps copy, merge, and pass values concisely.
spread-operator.whyOutput: spread expands both arrays into a new combined array.
spread-operator.mistake: using spread as if it deeply clones nested data
spread-operator.practice: use spread for first-level copy or merge operations

synchronous-vs-asynchronous.focus: the difference between code that finishes now and code that completes later
synchronous-vs-asynchronous.use: It explains why callbacks, Promises, and timers do not block normal execution.
synchronous-vs-asynchronous.whyOutput: the synchronous log runs immediately; the timer callback runs later.
synchronous-vs-asynchronous.mistake: expecting async results to be available on the next line
synchronous-vs-asynchronous.practice: use callbacks, then, or await when code depends on async results

this-keyword.focus: the value that represents the current calling context
this-keyword.use: It changes based on how a function is called.
this-keyword.whyOutput: calling show as user.show() sets this to user.
this-keyword.mistake: extracting a method and expecting this to stay connected automatically
this-keyword.practice: check the call site to understand this

throttling.focus: limiting a function so it runs at most once in a time window
throttling.use: It is useful for scroll, resize, and repeated pointer events.
throttling.whyOutput: a real throttle would allow one call per interval and skip or delay extras.
throttling.mistake: using debounce when regular updates during the action are required
throttling.practice: use throttling when updates should happen steadily but not too often

tricky-programs.focus: small programs designed around surprising JavaScript behavior
tricky-programs.use: They train careful reasoning about coercion, scope, async order, and references.
tricky-programs.whyOutput: objects and arrays are coerced to strings in these expression positions.
tricky-programs.mistake: memorizing outputs without learning the coercion or parsing rule
tricky-programs.practice: write the conversion steps before trusting the final answer

truthy-falsy-values.focus: how JavaScript treats values as true-like or false-like in conditions
truthy-falsy-values.use: It affects if statements, logical operators, and defaults.
truthy-falsy-values.whyOutput: Boolean removes falsy 0 and empty string while keeping truthy values.
truthy-falsy-values.mistake: treating all non-boolean values as if they compare the same way
truthy-falsy-values.practice: know the falsy list: false, 0, -0, 0n, empty string, null, undefined, NaN

web-apis.focus: browser-provided features outside the JavaScript language core
web-apis.use: They provide timers, DOM, fetch, storage, and events.
web-apis.example:
setTimeout(() => console.log("from Web API"), 0);
console.log("JS stack");
web-apis.whyOutput: the timer is handled by the environment and its callback returns later.
web-apis.mistake: thinking setTimeout or DOM APIs are part of the ECMAScript language itself
web-apis.practice: separate JavaScript language rules from browser-provided APIs

introduction-to-javascript.description: Learn what JavaScript is and why every website uses it.
introduction-to-javascript.englishExplanation: JavaScript is a programming language that runs in the browser (and on servers via Node.js) and lets you make web pages interactive — reacting to clicks, updating content without reloading, and talking to servers.
introduction-to-javascript.babaKiBhashaExplanation: JavaScript ek programming language hai jo browser ke andar chalti hai (aur Node.js ki wajah se server pe bhi). Isi se website interactive banti hai — jaise button click hona, page ka content bina reload kiye update hona, aur server se data mangwana.
introduction-to-javascript.babaKiBhashaBasicExample: console.log("Hello, duniya!"); // yeh line screen pe kuch nahi, console mein print karti hai
introduction-to-javascript.whyExists: JavaScript exists because static HTML pages couldn't respond to anything a user did — it was the missing piece that let browsers run actual logic.
introduction-to-javascript.babaKiBhashaWhyExists: JavaScript isliye bani kyunki static HTML page kuch bhi respond nahi kar sakta tha jab user kuch karta tha — yeh woh missing piece thi jisne browser ko actual logic chalane ki power di.
introduction-to-javascript.realLifeExample: Think of HTML as the skeleton of a page, CSS as its clothes, and JavaScript as its muscles — the part that actually makes it move and respond.
introduction-to-javascript.babaKiBhashaRealLifeExample: HTML ko page ka skeleton samjho, CSS ko uske kapde, aur JavaScript ko uski muscles — jo actually page ko move aur respond karwati hai.
introduction-to-javascript.syntax:
// JavaScript can run directly in a <script> tag or a .js file
console.log("any statement ends with a semicolon");
introduction-to-javascript.babaKiBhashaSyntax:
// JavaScript <script> tag mein ya .js file mein chal sakti hai
console.log("har statement semicolon se khatam hota hai");
introduction-to-javascript.dryRun: JavaScript runs top to bottom: it defines the list, then calls forEach, which runs the callback once per item, passing in the item and its position.
introduction-to-javascript.babaKiBhashaDryRun: JavaScript upar se neeche chalta hai: pehle list banti hai, fir forEach call hota hai, jo har item ke liye callback ek baar chalata hai, item aur uska position dono deta hai.
introduction-to-javascript.whyOutput: forEach hands each array element to the callback along with its index, and the template string stitches the number and name together.
introduction-to-javascript.babaKiBhashaWhyOutput: forEach har array element ko uske index ke saath callback ko deta hai, aur template string number aur naam ko jod deti hai.
introduction-to-javascript.commonMistakes: Confusing = (assignment) with === (comparison), and forgetting that JavaScript is single-threaded — long-running code blocks everything else.
introduction-to-javascript.babaKiBhashaCommonMistakes: = (assignment) aur === (comparison) mein confuse ho jaana, aur yeh bhool jaana ki JavaScript single-threaded hai — lamba chalne wala code baaki sab kuch block kar deta hai.
introduction-to-javascript.bestPractices: Use const by default, let only when a value needs to change, and never var. Keep functions small and name them by what they do.
introduction-to-javascript.babaKiBhashaBestPractices: Default mein const use karo, let sirf tab jab value change honi ho, aur var kabhi mat use karo. Functions ko chhota rakho aur unka naam waisa do jo woh karte hain.
introduction-to-javascript.interviewQuestions: What makes JavaScript different from a language like Java, and where can it run?
introduction-to-javascript.babaKiBhashaInterviewQuestions: JavaScript, Java jaisi language se kaise alag hai, aur yeh kahan-kahan chal sakti hai?
introduction-to-javascript.summary: JavaScript is the language that brings interactivity to the web, running in browsers and, via Node.js, on servers too.
introduction-to-javascript.babaKiBhashaSummary: JavaScript woh language hai jo web ko interactive banati hai — browser mein toh chalti hi hai, Node.js ki wajah se server pe bhi chalti hai.

execution-context.description: Learn what execution context means in JavaScript.
execution-context.englishExplanation: Execution context describes the environment where JavaScript code runs and the data it uses while executing.
execution-context.babaKiBhashaExplanation: Execution context ka matlab hai woh jagah jahan JavaScript ka code chalta hai aur jahan us code ka saara data (variables, functions) store hota hai jab tak woh chal raha hota hai.
execution-context.whyExists: This concept exists so you can predict how JavaScript runs code and avoid runtime surprises.
execution-context.babaKiBhashaWhyExists: Yeh concept isliye hai taaki tumhe pata chale JavaScript code ko kaise chalata hai, aur code chalte waqt koi surprise na aaye.
execution-context.realLifeExample: Think of it like a room where a task is being done and all the tools needed for that task are available.
execution-context.babaKiBhashaRealLifeExample: Isko aise samjho jaise ek kamra hai jahan koi kaam ho raha hai, aur us kaam ke liye jitne bhi tools chahiye woh sab wahin maujood hain.
execution-context.dryRun: JavaScript creates an environment for the function call and then executes the code inside it.
execution-context.babaKiBhashaDryRun: JavaScript function call ke liye ek environment banata hai, uske baad us environment ke andar ka code execute karta hai.
execution-context.whyOutput: The function uses the variable from its surrounding environment while executing.
execution-context.babaKiBhashaWhyOutput: Function apne aas-paas ke environment se variable ki value uthata hai jab woh chal raha hota hai.
execution-context.commonMistakes: Forgetting that variables are resolved based on the current execution environment.
execution-context.babaKiBhashaCommonMistakes: Yeh bhool jaana ki variables current execution environment ke hisaab se resolve hote hain.
execution-context.bestPractices: Keep variable scope clear and understand how each function call creates its own context.
execution-context.babaKiBhashaBestPractices: Variable scope ko clear rakho aur samjho ki har function call apna alag context banata hai.
execution-context.interviewQuestions: What is execution context and how does it affect variable access in JavaScript?
execution-context.babaKiBhashaInterviewQuestions: Execution context kya hota hai aur yeh JavaScript mein variable access ko kaise affect karta hai?
execution-context.summary: Execution context is the environment in which JavaScript code is evaluated and executed.
execution-context.babaKiBhashaSummary: Execution context woh environment hai jismein JavaScript code evaluate aur execute hota hai.

global-execution-context.description: Learn what the Global Execution Context is and what happens the moment your script starts running.
global-execution-context.englishExplanation: The Global Execution Context (GEC) is the base environment JavaScript creates the moment a file starts running — before any function is called. It sets up the global object (window in browsers, global in Node), creates the 'this' keyword at the top level, and does an initial pass to allocate memory for variables and functions.
global-execution-context.babaKiBhashaExplanation: Global Execution Context (GEC) woh sabse pehla environment hai jo JavaScript banata hai jab file run hona shuru hoti hai — kisi bhi function ke call hone se pehle. Isme global object banta hai (browser mein window, Node mein global), top level pe 'this' set hota hai, aur variables/functions ke liye memory pehle se allocate ho jaati hai.
global-execution-context.babaKiBhashaBasicExample:
console.log(this); // top level pe 'this' global object ko point karta hai
var naam = "Aditi"; // yeh global scope mein create hua
global-execution-context.whyExists: It exists because JavaScript needs somewhere to put top-level code and variables before any function runs — without a base context, there'd be nowhere for global state or the initial 'this' to live.
global-execution-context.babaKiBhashaWhyExists: Yeh isliye zaroori hai kyunki JavaScript ko kisi function ke chalne se pehle top-level code aur variables ko kahin rakhna hota hai — agar base context na ho toh global state aur shuruwati 'this' kahin exist hi nahi karenge.
global-execution-context.realLifeExample: Think of it as the foundation of a building being laid before any rooms (functions) are built — the foundation (GEC) exists first, and every room is constructed on top of it.
global-execution-context.babaKiBhashaRealLifeExample: Isko building ki foundation samjho jo kisi bhi room (function) ke banne se pehle daali jaati hai — foundation (GEC) pehle se maujood hoti hai, aur har room usi ke upar banta hai.
global-execution-context.syntax:
// Created automatically — there's no keyword to trigger it
// It exists the instant your script starts executing
global-execution-context.babaKiBhashaSyntax:
// Yeh automatically create hota hai — koi keyword iske liye nahi hai
// Script ke chalna shuru hote hi yeh exist karne lagta hai
global-execution-context.dryRun: JavaScript runs two phases inside the GEC: a memory creation phase, where it scans the code and allocates space for every var (set to undefined) and every function (stored in full), then the execution phase, where code actually runs line by line.
global-execution-context.babaKiBhashaDryRun: JavaScript GEC ke andar do phase chalata hai: memory creation phase, jahan code scan karke har var ke liye space allocate hota hai (undefined set hota hai) aur har function poora store ho jaata hai, uske baad execution phase, jahan code line by line actually chalta hai.
global-execution-context.whyOutput: Functions are hoisted with their entire body, so typeof greet is already "function" before the definition line runs. var is hoisted as a name only, so typeof value is "undefined" until the assignment line executes.
global-execution-context.babaKiBhashaWhyOutput: Functions apni poori body ke saath hoist hote hain, isliye typeof greet definition line se pehle hi "function" hota hai. var sirf naam ke roop mein hoist hota hai, isliye typeof value tab tak "undefined" rehta hai jab tak assignment line chal nahi jaati.
global-execution-context.commonMistakes: Assuming var behaves like let/const during hoisting, and forgetting that there is only ever one Global Execution Context per program — it's created once, not per function call.
global-execution-context.babaKiBhashaCommonMistakes: Yeh sochna ki var, hoisting ke time let/const jaisa behave karta hai, aur yeh bhool jaana ki poore program mein sirf ek hi Global Execution Context hota hai — yeh ek baar banta hai, har function call pe nahi.
global-execution-context.bestPractices: Avoid relying on hoisting — declare variables with let/const near where you use them, and declare functions before you call them for readability even though hoisting would technically allow otherwise.
global-execution-context.babaKiBhashaBestPractices: Hoisting pe bharosa mat karo — variables ko let/const se wahin declare karo jahan use karne wale ho, aur functions ko call karne se pehle hi declare karo, readability ke liye, chahe hoisting technically allow karta ho.
global-execution-context.interviewQuestions: What gets created during the Global Execution Context's memory phase, and how does that differ between var declarations and function declarations?
global-execution-context.babaKiBhashaInterviewQuestions: Global Execution Context ke memory phase mein kya create hota hai, aur var declarations aur function declarations mein yeh kaise alag hota hai?
global-execution-context.summary: The Global Execution Context is the first environment JavaScript sets up, giving you the global object, top-level 'this', and hoisted variables/functions before any code actually executes.
global-execution-context.babaKiBhashaSummary: Global Execution Context woh pehla environment hai jo JavaScript set up karta hai, jisme global object, top-level 'this', aur hoisted variables/functions milte hain — yeh sab actual code chalne se pehle hi ready hote hain.

function-execution-context.description: Learn what happens every time a function is called — and why each call gets its own fresh environment.
function-execution-context.englishExplanation: A Function Execution Context (FEC) is created every single time a function is invoked. Unlike the Global Execution Context, which exists once, a new FEC is pushed onto the call stack for each call — with its own arguments object, local variables, and 'this' binding — and popped off once the function returns.
function-execution-context.babaKiBhashaExplanation: Function Execution Context (FEC) har baar banta hai jab bhi koi function call hota hai. Global Execution Context ke ulat, jo sirf ek baar banta hai, har function call ke liye ek naya FEC call stack pe push hota hai — apna arguments object, local variables, aur 'this' binding ke saath — aur function return hote hi pop ho jaata hai.
function-execution-context.whyExists: It exists so that every function call gets an isolated workspace — local variables from one call never leak into or clash with another call, even for the same function running recursively or repeatedly.
function-execution-context.babaKiBhashaWhyExists: Yeh isliye zaroori hai taaki har function call ko apna alag workspace mile — ek call ke local variables kabhi doosre call mein leak ya clash nahi karte, chahe wahi function recursively ya baar-baar chal raha ho.
function-execution-context.realLifeExample: Think of each function call as checking into its own hotel room: you get a fresh, empty room every time you check in, and whatever you leave in it disappears once you check out — the next guest starts with an empty room too.
function-execution-context.babaKiBhashaRealLifeExample: Har function call ko ek hotel room mein check-in karne jaisa samjho: har baar check-in karte waqt tumhe ek naya, khaali room milta hai, aur check-out karte hi jo bhi usme chhoda tha woh gayab ho jaata hai — agla guest bhi khaali room se hi shuru karta hai.
function-execution-context.syntax:
// Created automatically on every function call
functionName(); // this call alone creates and later destroys one FEC
function-execution-context.babaKiBhashaSyntax:
// Har function call pe automatically banta hai
functionName(); // sirf yeh call ek FEC banata hai aur baad mein destroy kar deta hai
function-execution-context.dryRun: Calling outer() creates one FEC for outer, which sets count to 0. Each call to inner() creates its own FEC, but inner's FEC looks up count in outer's FEC via the scope chain, increments it, and logs it — so the second inner() call sees the value left behind by the first.
function-execution-context.babaKiBhashaDryRun: outer() call karne se outer ka ek FEC banta hai, jisme count 0 set hota hai. inner() ke har call se uska apna FEC banta hai, lekin inner ka FEC scope chain ke through outer ke FEC mein count ko dhoondta hai, use increment karta hai, aur log karta hai — isliye inner() ki dusri call pehli call ki chhodi hui value dekhti hai.
function-execution-context.whyOutput: count lives in outer's FEC, not inner's. Since both inner() calls share the same outer FEC through closure, the increments accumulate: 1 on the first call, 2 on the second.
function-execution-context.babaKiBhashaWhyOutput: count outer ke FEC mein rehta hai, inner ke nahi. Chunki dono inner() calls closure ke through wahi outer FEC share karte hain, increments jama hote jaate hain: pehli call pe 1, dusri call pe 2.
function-execution-context.commonMistakes: Assuming variables declared inside a function are visible outside it, and forgetting that each recursive call creates a brand-new FEC on the stack — recursion doesn't reuse the previous call's variables.
function-execution-context.babaKiBhashaCommonMistakes: Yeh sochna ki function ke andar declare kiye variables uske bahar bhi dikhte hain, aur yeh bhool jaana ki har recursive call stack pe ek bilkul naya FEC banata hai — recursion pichli call ke variables ko reuse nahi karta.
function-execution-context.bestPractices: Keep functions focused on their own local state, avoid relying on outer variables unless you intend a closure, and remember deep recursion pushes many FECs onto the stack, which can overflow it.
function-execution-context.babaKiBhashaBestPractices: Functions ko apne local state tak focused rakho, outer variables pe bharosa mat karo jab tak closure banana hi maksad na ho, aur yaad rakho ki deep recursion stack pe bahut saare FEC push kar deta hai, jo overflow kar sakta hai.
function-execution-context.interviewQuestions: How many Function Execution Contexts exist at once when a function calls itself recursively three times, and what happens to each one?
function-execution-context.babaKiBhashaInterviewQuestions: Agar ek function khud ko recursively teen baar call kare, toh ek saath kitne Function Execution Context exist karte hain, aur har ek ka kya hota hai?
function-execution-context.summary: A Function Execution Context is created fresh on every function call, giving that call its own arguments, local variables, and 'this', then gets popped off the call stack when the function finishes.
function-execution-context.babaKiBhashaSummary: Function Execution Context har function call pe naya banta hai, jo us call ko apne arguments, local variables, aur 'this' deta hai, aur function khatam hote hi call stack se pop ho jaata hai.

call-stack.description: Learn how JavaScript keeps track of which function is currently running.
call-stack.englishExplanation: The Call Stack is a LIFO (Last In, First Out) structure JavaScript uses to track execution contexts. The Global Execution Context sits at the bottom; every function call pushes a new Function Execution Context on top, and it's popped off the moment that function returns.
call-stack.babaKiBhashaExplanation: Call Stack ek LIFO (Last In, First Out) structure hai jisse JavaScript execution contexts track karta hai. Global Execution Context sabse neeche hota hai; har function call ek naya Function Execution Context upar push karta hai, aur function return hote hi woh pop ho jaata hai.
call-stack.whyExists: It exists so JavaScript always knows exactly which function to return to once the current one finishes — without it, nested and recursive calls would have no way of resuming the right caller.
call-stack.babaKiBhashaWhyExists: Yeh isliye zaroori hai taaki JavaScript ko hamesha pata rahe ki current function khatam hone ke baad kis function pe wapas jaana hai — iske bina nested aur recursive calls ko sahi caller pe resume karne ka koi tareeka nahi hota.
call-stack.realLifeExample: Think of a stack of plates: you can only add or remove from the top. When function A calls function B, B's plate goes on top of A's; B has to be cleared away before A can be picked back up.
call-stack.babaKiBhashaRealLifeExample: Plates ke stack jaisa samjho: tum sirf upar se plate daal ya nikaal sakte ho. Jab function A, function B ko call karta hai, toh B ki plate A ke upar rakhi jaati hai; B hatne ke baad hi A ko wapas uthaya ja sakta hai.
call-stack.syntax:
// No special syntax — every function call automatically uses the stack
functionName();
call-stack.babaKiBhashaSyntax:
// Koi special syntax nahi hai — har function call automatically stack use karta hai
functionName();
call-stack.dryRun: countdown(3) is pushed and logs 3, then calls countdown(2), pushing it on top; this repeats down to countdown(0), which logs 'done!' and returns. Each return pops that call off, unwinding the stack back down to countdown(3), which then also returns.
call-stack.babaKiBhashaDryRun: countdown(3) push hota hai aur 3 log karta hai, fir countdown(2) ko call karta hai, jo upar push hota hai; yeh countdown(0) tak repeat hota hai, jo 'done!' log karke return karta hai. Har return us call ko pop kar deta hai, stack unwind hoke countdown(3) tak wapas aata hai, jo woh bhi return karta hai.
call-stack.whyOutput: Each call logs its number before recursing deeper, so the numbers print in descending order as the stack grows, and 'done!' only appears once the base case (n <= 0) is reached at the top of the stack.
call-stack.babaKiBhashaWhyOutput: Har call apna number log karta hai deeper recurse hone se pehle, isliye numbers descending order mein print hote hain jaise-jaise stack badhta hai, aur 'done!' sirf tab aata hai jab base case (n <= 0) stack ke top pe pahunchta hai.
call-stack.commonMistakes: Writing recursion without a proper base case, which keeps pushing new frames until you hit 'Maximum call stack size exceeded', and confusing the call stack (execution tracking) with the heap (where objects are stored).
call-stack.babaKiBhashaCommonMistakes: Recursion likhte waqt proper base case na dena, jiski wajah se naye frames push hote rehte hain jab tak 'Maximum call stack size exceeded' error na aaye, aur call stack (execution tracking) ko heap (jahan objects store hote hain) se confuse karna.
call-stack.bestPractices: Always give recursive functions a clear base case, keep recursion depth reasonable or convert to an iterative loop for deep operations, and use browser/Node dev tools' call stack panel when debugging to see the exact chain of calls.
call-stack.babaKiBhashaBestPractices: Recursive functions ko hamesha clear base case do, recursion depth reasonable rakho ya deep operations ke liye iterative loop mein convert karo, aur debugging ke time browser/Node dev tools ke call stack panel ka use karo taaki calls ki exact chain dikhe.
call-stack.interviewQuestions: What causes a 'Maximum call stack size exceeded' error, and how would you fix a function that triggers it?
call-stack.babaKiBhashaInterviewQuestions: 'Maximum call stack size exceeded' error kis wajah se aata hai, aur jo function isse trigger karta hai use kaise fix karoge?
call-stack.summary: The Call Stack tracks which execution context is currently active using a last-in-first-out order, pushing a new frame on every call and popping it off on every return.
call-stack.babaKiBhashaSummary: Call Stack yeh track karta hai ki currently kaunsa execution context active hai, last-in-first-out order use karke — har call pe ek naya frame push hota hai aur har return pe woh pop ho jaata hai.

hoisting.description: Learn why some variables and functions seem to be usable before they're declared.
hoisting.englishExplanation: Hoisting is the result of JavaScript's memory creation phase: before any code runs, the engine scans the current scope and reserves space for var declarations (set to undefined) and function declarations (stored with their full body). It looks like declarations 'moved to the top', but nothing physically moves — the engine just processes them early.
hoisting.babaKiBhashaExplanation: Hoisting JavaScript ke memory creation phase ka result hai: kisi bhi code ke chalne se pehle, engine current scope scan karta hai aur var declarations ke liye space reserve karta hai (undefined set karke) aur function declarations ko unki poori body ke saath store kar leta hai. Aisa lagta hai jaise declarations 'upar move' ho gaye, lekin actually kuch move nahi hota — engine bas unhe pehle process kar leta hai.
hoisting.babaKiBhashaBasicExample:
console.log(a); // undefined — 'a' hoist ho gaya, value nahi
var a = 5;
hoisting.whyExists: Hoisting exists as a side effect of how the engine builds the execution context in two phases — it isn't a feature you 'use' on purpose, but understanding it prevents confusing bugs around variable and function order.
hoisting.babaKiBhashaWhyExists: Hoisting engine ke do phases mein execution context banane ka ek side effect hai — yeh koi feature nahi hai jise tum jaanbhoojh kar 'use' karte ho, lekin ise samajhna variable aur function order ke around confusing bugs se bachata hai.
hoisting.realLifeExample: It's like a venue reserving seats with name cards before the event starts (memory phase) — the seat exists and has your name on it, but you can't actually sit and use it (get the real value) until you physically arrive (execution phase).
hoisting.babaKiBhashaRealLifeExample: Isko aisa samjho jaise event shuru hone se pehle venue name cards ke saath seats reserve kar deta hai (memory phase) — seat exist karti hai aur uspe naam bhi hai, lekin tum us seat ko actually use nahi kar sakte (real value nahi mil sakti) jab tak tum physically pahuncho (execution phase).
hoisting.syntax:
// var and function declarations are hoisted
// let/const are hoisted too, but stay in the Temporal Dead Zone until declared
hoisting.babaKiBhashaSyntax:
// var aur function declarations hoist hote hain
// let/const bhi hoist hote hain, lekin declare hone tak Temporal Dead Zone mein rehte hain
hoisting.dryRun: During the memory phase, num is reserved and set to undefined, and greetFn is stored as a full function. During execution, the two typeof checks run before the var num = 10 line, so num still reads as undefined while greetFn is already callable.
hoisting.babaKiBhashaDryRun: Memory phase mein, num reserve hota hai aur undefined set hota hai, aur greetFn poore function ke roop mein store hota hai. Execution ke time, dono typeof checks var num = 10 line se pehle chalte hain, isliye num abhi bhi undefined hi read hota hai jabki greetFn already callable hai.
hoisting.whyOutput: Only the name of a var is hoisted, not its assigned value, so typeof num is 'undefined' at that point. Function declarations hoist with their entire definition, so typeof greetFn is already 'function'.
hoisting.babaKiBhashaWhyOutput: var ka sirf naam hoist hota hai, uski assigned value nahi, isliye us point pe typeof num 'undefined' hai. Function declarations apni poori definition ke saath hoist hote hain, isliye typeof greetFn already 'function' hai.
hoisting.commonMistakes: Thinking a variable declared with var has its value available before the assignment line runs, and trying to call a function stored in a var/let/const before that line executes (function expressions and arrow functions are not hoisted like function declarations).
hoisting.babaKiBhashaCommonMistakes: Yeh sochna ki var se declare kiya variable, assignment line chalne se pehle bhi apni value deta hai, aur var/let/const mein store kiye function ko us line ke execute hone se pehle call karne ki koshish karna (function expressions aur arrow functions, function declarations jaisa hoist nahi hote).
hoisting.bestPractices: Declare variables and functions before using them regardless of hoisting rules — write code in the order it actually needs to run so readers don't have to think about hoisting at all.
hoisting.babaKiBhashaBestPractices: Hoisting rules chahe jo bhi ho, variables aur functions ko use karne se pehle hi declare karo — code ko us order mein likho jisme woh actually chalna chahiye, taaki reader ko hoisting ke baare mein sochna hi na pade.
hoisting.interviewQuestions: Why does console.log(typeof someVar) print 'undefined' instead of throwing an error when someVar is declared with var later in the same scope?
hoisting.babaKiBhashaInterviewQuestions: console.log(typeof someVar) error kyun nahi deta aur 'undefined' print karta hai, jab someVar usi scope mein baad mein var se declare hua ho?
hoisting.summary: Hoisting means variable and function declarations are processed during the memory creation phase, before code executes — var gets set to undefined, functions get their full body, and let/const remain inaccessible until their declaration line.
hoisting.babaKiBhashaSummary: Hoisting ka matlab hai ki variable aur function declarations memory creation phase ke dauraan process hote hain, code chalne se pehle — var undefined set hota hai, functions ko unki poori body milti hai, aur let/const apni declaration line tak inaccessible rehte hain.

temporal-dead-zone-tdz.description: Learn why accessing a let or const variable too early throws an error instead of returning undefined.
temporal-dead-zone-tdz.englishExplanation: The Temporal Dead Zone is the span of code between entering a scope and the line where a let or const variable is actually declared. The variable exists in memory during this span (it's hoisted), but it can't be read or written — trying to access it throws a ReferenceError instead of silently returning undefined.
temporal-dead-zone-tdz.babaKiBhashaExplanation: Temporal Dead Zone woh code ka hissa hai jo scope mein enter karne aur us line ke beech mein aata hai jahan let ya const variable actually declare hota hai. Is span mein variable memory mein exist karta hai (hoist ho chuka hota hai), lekin use read ya write nahi kiya ja sakta — access karne ki koshish karne pe ReferenceError aata hai, chup chaap undefined nahi milta.
temporal-dead-zone-tdz.babaKiBhashaBasicExample:
console.log(score); // ReferenceError — TDZ ke andar hai
let score = 100;
temporal-dead-zone-tdz.whyExists: The TDZ exists to catch a real class of bugs — reading a variable before its declaration line almost always signals a logic mistake, so ES6 made it a hard error instead of letting var's silent 'undefined' behavior hide the problem.
temporal-dead-zone-tdz.babaKiBhashaWhyExists: TDZ isliye hai taaki ek real bug pakda ja sake — declaration line se pehle variable read karna almost hamesha ek logic mistake ko signal karta hai, isliye ES6 ne ise ek hard error banaya, na ki var jaisa chup chaap 'undefined' dekar problem chhupane diya.
temporal-dead-zone-tdz.realLifeExample: It's like a parcel that has already arrived at the delivery office (it exists) but is locked in a cage until the recipient's name is officially registered — you can see it's there, but you cannot touch it yet.
temporal-dead-zone-tdz.babaKiBhashaRealLifeExample: Isko aise samjho jaise ek parcel delivery office mein pahuch chuka hai (exist karta hai) lekin cage mein locked hai jab tak recipient ka naam officially register nahi ho jaata — tumhe dikhta hai ki woh wahan hai, lekin abhi tum use chhoo nahi sakte.
temporal-dead-zone-tdz.syntax:
// TDZ applies automatically to every let and const
// It ends the moment the declaration line executes
temporal-dead-zone-tdz.babaKiBhashaSyntax:
// TDZ automatically har let aur const pe apply hota hai
// Yeh declaration line execute hote hi khatam ho jaata hai
temporal-dead-zone-tdz.dryRun: Entering checkTemp's scope hoists threshold but leaves it in the TDZ. The if-condition tries to read threshold before its declaration line has run, so the engine throws a ReferenceError right there — the function never even reaches the console.log.
temporal-dead-zone-tdz.babaKiBhashaDryRun: checkTemp ke scope mein enter karte hi threshold hoist ho jaata hai lekin TDZ mein rehta hai. if-condition threshold ko uski declaration line chalne se pehle hi read karne ki koshish karta hai, isliye engine wahin ReferenceError throw kar deta hai — function console.log tak pahuchta hi nahi.
temporal-dead-zone-tdz.whyOutput: threshold is in the TDZ at the point the if-condition runs because its let declaration hasn't executed yet in this call, so any read attempt before that line throws instead of returning undefined.
temporal-dead-zone-tdz.babaKiBhashaWhyOutput: if-condition chalne ke waqt threshold TDZ mein hota hai kyunki uski let declaration is call mein abhi tak execute nahi hui, isliye us line se pehle koi bhi read attempt undefined return karne ki jagah error throw karta hai.
temporal-dead-zone-tdz.commonMistakes: Assuming let/const behave exactly like var except for scoping, and not realizing that the TDZ applies per-scope — re-entering a loop or block creates a brand-new TDZ for that iteration's let variable.
temporal-dead-zone-tdz.babaKiBhashaCommonMistakes: Yeh sochna ki let/const scoping ke alawa bilkul var jaisa hi behave karte hain, aur yeh na samajhna ki TDZ per-scope apply hota hai — loop ya block mein dobara enter karna us iteration ke let variable ke liye ek bilkul naya TDZ banata hai.
temporal-dead-zone-tdz.bestPractices: Declare let/const variables at the top of the block they're used in so there's no gap for a TDZ-related error to occur, and treat a 'Cannot access before initialization' error as a sign to reorder your declarations, not to switch back to var.
temporal-dead-zone-tdz.babaKiBhashaBestPractices: let/const variables ko us block ke top pe declare karo jahan woh use ho rahe hain, taaki TDZ-related error ke liye koi gap na bache, aur 'Cannot access before initialization' error ko declarations reorder karne ka signal samjho, var pe wapas jaane ka nahi.
temporal-dead-zone-tdz.interviewQuestions: Why does accessing a let variable before its declaration throw a ReferenceError, while accessing a var in the same situation returns undefined?
temporal-dead-zone-tdz.babaKiBhashaInterviewQuestions: let variable ko uski declaration se pehle access karna ReferenceError kyun deta hai, jabki isi situation mein var access karne pe undefined milta hai?
temporal-dead-zone-tdz.summary: The Temporal Dead Zone is the window where a hoisted let or const variable exists but can't be accessed, closing the moment its declaration line runs — a deliberate safeguard against reading variables before they're meant to be used.
temporal-dead-zone-tdz.babaKiBhashaSummary: Temporal Dead Zone woh window hai jahan hoist ho chuka let ya const variable exist toh karta hai lekin access nahi kiya ja sakta, jo uski declaration line chalte hi band ho jaata hai — yeh ek jaanbhoojh kar diya gaya safeguard hai taaki variables use se pehle na padhe jaayein.

var-vs-let-vs-const.description: Learn how the three ways of declaring a variable differ in scope, reassignment, and hoisting.
var-vs-let-vs-const.englishExplanation: var is function-scoped, hoisted with an undefined default, and can be redeclared and reassigned. let is block-scoped, hoisted into the TDZ, and can be reassigned but not redeclared. const is block-scoped like let, cannot be reassigned after its initial value, but note that objects/arrays assigned to a const can still have their contents mutated.
var-vs-let-vs-const.babaKiBhashaExplanation: var function-scoped hota hai, undefined default ke saath hoist hota hai, aur usse redeclare aur reassign dono kiya ja sakta hai. let block-scoped hota hai, TDZ mein hoist hota hai, reassign ho sakta hai lekin redeclare nahi. const bhi let jaisa block-scoped hota hai, apni initial value ke baad reassign nahi ho sakta, lekin agar const mein object/array store hai toh uska content mutate ho sakta hai.
var-vs-let-vs-const.whyExists: let and const were introduced in ES6 specifically to fix var's confusing function-scoping and accidental redeclaration, giving developers block scope and, with const, a way to signal 'this binding won't change'.
var-vs-let-vs-const.babaKiBhashaWhyExists: let aur const ES6 mein isliye laaye gaye taaki var ki confusing function-scoping aur accidental redeclaration ki problem fix ho sake, developers ko block scope mile aur const ke saath yeh signal mile ki 'yeh binding change nahi hogi'.
var-vs-let-vs-const.realLifeExample: var is like leaving your stuff anywhere in a shared house — it's visible to the whole house (function scope). let and const are like keeping your stuff in your own locked room — visible only inside that room (block scope).
var-vs-let-vs-const.babaKiBhashaRealLifeExample: var ko aise samjho jaise tum apna saaman shared house mein kahin bhi rakh do — poore ghar (function scope) ko dikhta hai. let aur const ko apne locked room mein saaman rakhne jaisa samjho — sirf usi room (block scope) ke andar dikhta hai.
var-vs-let-vs-const.dryRun: The var loop finishes all three iterations first, leaving one shared i at value 3, so every delayed callback reads that same final value. The let loop creates a fresh j binding for each iteration, so each callback captures its own snapshot: 0, 1, 2.
var-vs-let-vs-const.babaKiBhashaDryRun: var wala loop pehle teeno iterations poore kar leta hai, ek hi shared i value 3 pe chhod deta hai, isliye har delayed callback wahi final value padhta hai. let wala loop har iteration ke liye ek naya j binding banata hai, isliye har callback apna snapshot capture karta hai: 0, 1, 2.
var-vs-let-vs-const.whyOutput: Because var is function-scoped, all three setTimeout callbacks close over the same variable, which has already reached 3 by the time they run. Because let is block-scoped, each loop iteration gets its own variable, so each callback keeps the value it saw at that iteration.
var-vs-let-vs-const.babaKiBhashaWhyOutput: var function-scoped hai, isliye teeno setTimeout callbacks usi variable ko close over karte hain, jo unke chalne tak already 3 tak pahunch chuka hota hai. let block-scoped hai, isliye har loop iteration ko apna variable milta hai, isliye har callback apni iteration ki value rakhta hai.
var-vs-let-vs-const.commonMistakes: Using var inside loops with async callbacks and being surprised all callbacks see the final value, and trying to reassign a const, which throws 'Assignment to constant variable' even though mutating a const object's properties is perfectly fine.
var-vs-let-vs-const.babaKiBhashaCommonMistakes: Loops ke andar async callbacks ke saath var use karna aur surprise hona ki sab callbacks final value dekhte hain, aur const ko reassign karne ki koshish karna, jo 'Assignment to constant variable' error deta hai, jabki const object ki properties mutate karna bilkul theek hai.
var-vs-let-vs-const.bestPractices: Default to const for anything that won't be reassigned, use let only when a value genuinely needs to change, and avoid var entirely in modern code — it rarely offers any benefit over let/const.
var-vs-let-vs-const.babaKiBhashaBestPractices: Default mein const use karo jo cheez reassign nahi hogi, let sirf tab use karo jab value ko genuinely change hona ho, aur modern code mein var bilkul avoid karo — yeh let/const se zyada koi fayda shayad hi deta hai.
var-vs-let-vs-const.interviewQuestions: Why does a var-based for loop with setTimeout print the same final value for every callback, while a let-based loop prints each iteration's own value?
var-vs-let-vs-const.babaKiBhashaInterviewQuestions: var-based for loop setTimeout ke saath har callback ke liye same final value kyun print karta hai, jabki let-based loop har iteration ki apni value print karta hai?
var-vs-let-vs-const.summary: var is function-scoped and loosely enforced, let is block-scoped and reassignable, and const is block-scoped and locked to its initial binding — modern JavaScript favors const first, let when needed, and var almost never.
var-vs-let-vs-const.babaKiBhashaSummary: var function-scoped hai aur loosely enforce hota hai, let block-scoped hai aur reassignable hai, aur const block-scoped hai aur apni initial binding pe locked hai — modern JavaScript pehle const, zaroorat pe let, aur var almost kabhi nahi prefer karta hai.

data-types.description: Learn the different kinds of values JavaScript can store and how typeof helps you inspect them.
data-types.englishExplanation: Data types describe what kind of value a variable is holding. JavaScript has primitive types like string, number, boolean, undefined, null, bigint, and symbol, plus non-primitive values like objects, arrays, and functions. Primitive values are copied by value, while objects are copied by reference.
data-types.babaKiBhashaExplanation: Data types batate hain ki variable ke andar kis type ki value store hai. JavaScript mein primitive types hote hain jaise string, number, boolean, undefined, null, bigint, symbol, aur non-primitive values hote hain jaise objects, arrays, functions. Primitive values value se copy hoti hain, objects reference se copy hote hain.
data-types.babaKiBhashaBasicExample:
let name = "Baba"; // string
let age = 25; // number
let isLearning = true; // boolean
let score; // undefined
let empty = null; // intentionally empty
data-types.babaKiBhashaMediumExample:
console.log(typeof "Baba"); // "string"
console.log(typeof 99); // "number"
console.log(typeof true); // "boolean"
console.log(typeof undefined); // "undefined"
console.log(typeof null); // "object" - old JavaScript quirk
data-types.whyExists: Data types exist so the JavaScript engine knows how to store, compare, convert, and operate on values. Adding two numbers, joining two strings, and updating an object all require different behavior.
data-types.babaKiBhashaWhyExists: Data types isliye hote hain taaki JavaScript engine samajh sake value ko store, compare, convert, aur operate kaise karna hai. Do numbers add karna, do strings join karna, aur object update karna sabka behavior alag hota hai.
data-types.realLifeExample: Think of data types like labeled boxes: a money box stores numbers, a name tag stores text, a yes/no switch stores booleans, and a profile folder stores many related details together.
data-types.babaKiBhashaRealLifeExample: Data types ko labeled boxes jaisa samjho: money box numbers rakhta hai, name tag text rakhta hai, yes/no switch boolean rakhta hai, aur profile folder ek saath multiple details store karta hai.
data-types.dryRun: First, x holds the primitive number 10. y receives a copy of that value, so changing y to 20 does not affect x. Then user holds an object reference. copy receives that same reference, so changing copy.name updates the same object that user points to.
data-types.babaKiBhashaDryRun: Pehle x primitive number 10 hold karta hai. y ko us value ki copy milti hai, isliye y ko 20 karne se x change nahi hota. Fir user ek object reference hold karta hai. copy ko wahi same reference milta hai, isliye copy.name change karne se wahi object update hota hai jise user point kar raha hai.
data-types.whyOutput: Primitive numbers are copied independently, so x stays 10. Objects are shared through references, so copy and user both point to the same object and user.name reflects the mutation.
data-types.babaKiBhashaWhyOutput: Primitive numbers independently copy hote hain, isliye x 10 hi rehta hai. Objects references ke through share hote hain, isliye copy aur user dono same object ko point karte hain aur user.name mutation dikha deta hai.
data-types.commonMistakes: Treating null and undefined as the same thing, trusting typeof null even though it returns 'object', and assuming object assignment creates a fresh copy when it actually copies the reference.
data-types.babaKiBhashaCommonMistakes: null aur undefined ko same samajhna, typeof null pe blindly trust karna jabki woh 'object' return karta hai, aur yeh assume karna ki object assignment fresh copy banata hai jabki woh reference copy karta hai.
data-types.bestPractices: Use null when you intentionally mean 'empty', let undefined mean 'not assigned yet', use Array.isArray for arrays, and be careful when copying objects or arrays because assignment does not deep-copy them.
data-types.babaKiBhashaBestPractices: Jab intentionally empty value chahiye tab null use karo, undefined ko 'abhi assign nahi hua' ke liye rehne do, arrays ke liye Array.isArray use karo, aur objects/arrays copy karte waqt careful raho kyunki assignment deep copy nahi banata.
data-types.interviewQuestions: What is the difference between primitive and non-primitive data types in JavaScript, and why does changing one object reference affect another variable?
data-types.babaKiBhashaInterviewQuestions: JavaScript mein primitive aur non-primitive data types mein kya difference hai, aur ek object reference change karne se doosra variable affect kyun hota hai?
data-types.summary: JavaScript data types define the shape of values: primitives are simple and copied by value, while objects, arrays, and functions are reference-based and can hold complex data.
data-types.babaKiBhashaSummary: JavaScript data types values ka shape define karte hain: primitives simple hote hain aur value se copy hote hain, jabki objects, arrays, functions reference-based hote hain aur complex data hold kar sakte hain.

type-conversion.description: Learn how JavaScript changes values from one data type to another, both automatically and manually.
type-conversion.englishExplanation: Type conversion means changing a value from one type to another, like string to number or boolean to string. JavaScript can do this implicitly during operations, or you can do it explicitly with helpers like Number(), String(), and Boolean().
type-conversion.babaKiBhashaExplanation: Type conversion ka matlab hai value ko ek type se doosre type mein badalna, jaise string se number ya boolean se string. JavaScript kabhi-kabhi operations ke time ye kaam khud kar deta hai, aur kabhi tum Number(), String(), Boolean() jaise helpers se manually karte ho.
type-conversion.babaKiBhashaMediumExample:
console.log("5" + 2); // "52" - number string ban gaya
console.log("5" - 2); // 3 - string number ban gayi
console.log(Boolean("hello")); // true
console.log(Boolean("")); // false
type-conversion.whyExists: Type conversion exists because JavaScript is dynamically typed. Values often come from forms, APIs, URLs, or storage as strings, but your program may need numbers, booleans, or other types to do real work.
type-conversion.babaKiBhashaWhyExists: Type conversion isliye exist karta hai kyunki JavaScript dynamically typed hai. Values forms, APIs, URLs, ya storage se aksar strings ke form mein aati hain, lekin program ko kaam karne ke liye numbers, booleans, ya doosre types chahiye hote hain.
type-conversion.realLifeExample: It's like reading a price written on paper. The text '500' is just writing until you treat it as an actual amount of money and calculate with it.
type-conversion.babaKiBhashaRealLifeExample: Isko price paper pe likhe hone jaisa samjho. Text '500' bas likha hua text hai, jab tak tum usse actual amount maan ke calculation nahi karte.
type-conversion.dryRun: Number('20 items') tries to convert the whole string into a valid number. Because the text contains non-number characters, the result is NaN. parseInt('20 items') reads from the start, takes the numeric part 20, and stops when it reaches text.
type-conversion.babaKiBhashaDryRun: Number('20 items') poori string ko valid number mein convert karne ki koshish karta hai. Kyunki text mein non-number characters hain, result NaN hota hai. parseInt('20 items') start se read karta hai, numeric part 20 leta hai, aur text aate hi ruk jaata hai.
type-conversion.whyOutput: Number() is strict about the whole value being numeric, while parseInt() extracts an integer from the beginning of the string when possible.
type-conversion.babaKiBhashaWhyOutput: Number() strict hota hai ki poori value numeric honi chahiye, jabki parseInt() possible ho toh string ke start se integer extract kar leta hai.
type-conversion.commonMistakes: Expecting + to always add numbers even when one side is a string, forgetting that Number('') becomes 0, and treating NaN as equal to itself instead of checking with Number.isNaN().
type-conversion.babaKiBhashaCommonMistakes: + operator ko hamesha number addition samajhna jabki ek side string ho sakti hai, yeh bhool jaana ki Number('') 0 ban jaata hai, aur NaN ko normal equality se check karna instead of Number.isNaN().
type-conversion.bestPractices: Convert user input explicitly before calculations, prefer Number() when the whole value must be numeric, use parseInt/parseFloat only when extracting numbers from mixed text is intentional, and avoid relying on clever implicit conversion.
type-conversion.babaKiBhashaBestPractices: Calculations se pehle user input ko explicitly convert karo, jab poori value numeric honi chahiye tab Number() prefer karo, parseInt/parseFloat tabhi use karo jab mixed text se number extract karna intentional ho, aur clever implicit conversion pe rely mat karo.
type-conversion.interviewQuestions: What is the difference between implicit and explicit type conversion, and why does '5' + 2 produce '52' while '5' - 2 produces 3?
type-conversion.babaKiBhashaInterviewQuestions: Implicit aur explicit type conversion mein kya difference hai, aur '5' + 2 se '52' kyun aata hai jabki '5' - 2 se 3 kyun aata hai?
type-conversion.summary: Type conversion changes values between types; explicit conversion is clearer and safer, while implicit conversion can be useful but often causes surprising JavaScript outputs.
type-conversion.babaKiBhashaSummary: Type conversion values ko types ke beech change karta hai; explicit conversion zyada clear aur safe hota hai, jabki implicit conversion useful ho sakta hai lekin aksar surprising JavaScript outputs deta hai.
